annotations.label: Least common multiple of two integers

anchor.snippet: is the smallest positive integer that is divisible by both a and b

annotations.note: Int.lcm (with Int.lcm_def = Nat.lcm of natAbs) is exactly the least common multiple function for integers, characterized as the least element in the divisibility order via dvd_lcm/lcm_dvd.

annotations.label: Convention lcm(a,0)=0

anchor.snippet: some authors define lcm( a , 0) as 0 for all a

annotations.note: lcm_zero_right/lcm_zero_left (and Nat.lcm_zero_right) encode exactly the convention lcm(a,0)=0=lcm(0,a).

annotations.label: Lowest common denominator

anchor.snippet: The least common multiple of the denominators of two fractions

annotations.note: Mathlib has no named 'lowest common denominator' definition; Rat arithmetic does not expose the lcm of denominators as a concept.

annotations.label: LCM of more than two integers

anchor.snippet: is defined as the smallest positive integer that is divisible by each of

annotations.note: Finset.lcm (and Multiset.lcm) generalize lcm to finitely many elements with Finset.lcm_dvd characterizing the least common multiple of the family.

annotations.label: Multiple of a number

anchor.snippet: A multiple of a number is the product of that number and an integer.

annotations.note: dvd_def states a ∣ b ↔ ∃ c, b = a * c, i.e. b is a multiple of a exactly when it is a's product with another element.

annotations.label: lcm(5,2)=10

anchor.snippet: it is the least common multiple of 5 and 2

annotations.note: This specific numeric evaluation is decidable (norm_num/decide) but is not stated as a named declaration in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Notation lcm(a,b)

anchor.snippet: The least common multiple of two integers a and b is denoted as lcm

annotations.note: Mathlib denotes the least common multiple by the function lcm a b (GCDMonoid.lcm), matching the article's notation.

annotations.label: lcm(4,6)=12

anchor.snippet: the smallest number is 12. Hence, the least common multiple is 12

annotations.note: A concrete computation provable by decide/norm_num, not present as a Mathlib lemma.

annotations.label: LCM as lowest common denominator

anchor.snippet: the least common multiple of the denominators (often called the lowest common denominator ) is used

annotations.note: Mathlib's fraction addition does not use a formalized lowest-common-denominator construction.

annotations.label: Gears problem

anchor.snippet: Suppose there are two meshing gears in a machine

annotations.note: An applied word problem with no corresponding Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Planetary alignment

anchor.snippet: Suppose there are three planets revolving around a star

annotations.note: An applied word problem with no corresponding Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: LCM from GCD formula

anchor.snippet: The least common multiple can be computed from the greatest common divisor

annotations.note: gcd_mul_lcm gives gcd a b * lcm a b ~ a * b (equality Nat.gcd_mul_lcm for ℕ), which is the lcm = ab/gcd relation.

annotations.label: Equivalent LCM-GCD formulas

anchor.snippet: it is convenient to use the equivalent formulas

annotations.note: The various equivalent forms are algebraic rearrangements of gcd_mul_lcm (gcd·lcm = product), which is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: LCM-GCD formula with zero arguments

anchor.snippet: These formulas are also valid when exactly one of a and b is 0

annotations.note: gcd_mul_lcm holds unconditionally including zero arguments, and lcm_zero_left/right handle the zero cases directly.

annotations.label: Unique factorization theorem

anchor.snippet: every positive integer greater than 1 can be written in only one way as a product of prime numbers

annotations.note: Nat.primeFactorsList_unique gives uniqueness and Nat.prod_primeFactorsList gives existence of the prime factorization of a positive integer.

annotations.label: lcm(8,9,21)

anchor.snippet: Example: lcm(8,9,21)

annotations.note: A concrete numeric example, not stated as a Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: LCM via highest prime powers

anchor.snippet: The lcm will be the product of multiplying the highest power of each prime number together.

annotations.note: Nat.factorization_lcm shows (lcm a b).factorization = a.factorization ⊔ b.factorization, i.e. the lcm takes the maximum (highest) exponent of each prime.

annotations.label: Venn diagram lcm(48,180)=720

anchor.snippet: Least common multiple = 2 × 2 × 2 × 2 × 3 × 3 × 5 = 720

annotations.note: A concrete numeric illustration, not present as a Mathlib lemma.

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of arithmetic

anchor.snippet: every integer greater than 1 can be represented uniquely as a product of prime numbers

annotations.note: The fundamental theorem of arithmetic is Nat.primeFactorsList_unique (uniqueness) together with Nat.prod_primeFactorsList, generalized abstractly by UniqueFactorizationMonoid.factors_unique.

annotations.label: GCD and LCM from prime exponents

anchor.snippet: their greatest common divisor and least common multiple are given by the formulas

annotations.note: Nat.factorization_lcm (sup/max of exponents) and Nat.factorization_gcd (inf/min of exponents) give gcd and lcm via prime exponents.

annotations.label: Prime factorization of rationals

anchor.snippet: every rational number can be written uniquely as the product of primes, if negative exponents are allowed

annotations.note: Mathlib has no factorization of rationals into primes with integer (negative-allowed) exponents.

annotations.label: Divisibility partial order

anchor.snippet: The positive integers may be partially ordered by divisibility

annotations.note: The divisibility partial order is formalized as Associates.instPartialOrder (≤ is divisibility), with antisymmetry on ℕ also available via Nat.dvd_antisymm.

annotations.label: Positive integers form a lattice

anchor.snippet: the positive integers become a lattice , with meet given by the gcd and join given by the lcm

annotations.note: The Lattice (Associates α) instance makes elements of a UFM (e.g. ℕ up to units) a lattice with inf = gcd and sup = lcm (cf. Associates.sup_mul_inf).

annotations.label: GCD-LCM duality principle

anchor.snippet: If a formula involving integer variables, gcd, lcm, ≤ and ≥ is true

annotations.note: This meta-mathematical order-duality principle is not formalized as a theorem in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dual lattice-theoretic identities

anchor.snippet: The following pairs of dual formulas are special cases of general lattice-theoretic identities.

annotations.note: General lattice identities (absorption, associativity, etc.) live in Mathlib.Order.Lattice and apply to the Associates gcd/lcm lattice, but the specific dual gcd/lcm formula pairs are not individually stated.

annotations.label: Distributivity of lcm and gcd

anchor.snippet: this lattice is distributive ; that is, lcm distributes over gcd and gcd distributes over lcm

annotations.note: The Associates gcd/lcm structure is only registered as a Lattice (not DistribLattice), and no gcd-over-lcm distributivity lemma was found, so distributivity itself is not formalized.

annotations.label: Self-dual distributive identity

anchor.snippet: This identity is self-dual

annotations.note: The self-dual median identity relating triple gcds and lcms is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: LCM over squarefree D

anchor.snippet: Let D be the product of

annotations.note: This specialized number-theoretic identity for squarefree D is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: LCM identity for nonzero arguments

anchor.snippet: is zero, then

annotations.note: The two-variable nonzero relation lcm·gcd = product is gcd_mul_lcm, but the multi-argument lcm-gcd identity in this section is not formalized.

annotations.label: Common multiple in a commutative ring

anchor.snippet: A common multiple of a and b is an element m of R such that both a and b divide m

annotations.note: The divisibility relation (Dvd/dvd_def) is fully available so 'a ∣ m ∧ b ∣ m' is expressible, but Mathlib has no standalone 'common multiple' definition.

annotations.label: Least common multiple in a commutative ring

anchor.snippet: A least common multiple of a and b is a common multiple that is minimal

annotations.note: lcm_dvd_iff (lcm a b ∣ c ↔ a ∣ c ∧ b ∣ c) captures the minimal-common-multiple characterization, but only in a GCDMonoid rather than an arbitrary commutative ring.

annotations.label: Least common multiples are associates

anchor.snippet: any two least common multiples of the same pair of elements are associates

annotations.note: associated_of_dvd_dvd yields that mutually-dividing elements are associates (so two minimal common multiples are associated), though Mathlib has no explicit 'is a least common multiple' predicate.

annotations.label: LCM exists in a UFD

anchor.snippet: In a unique factorization domain , any two elements have a least common multiple

annotations.note: UniqueFactorizationMonoid.toGCDMonoid endows any UFM with a GCDMonoid structure, providing an lcm (and gcd) for every pair of elements.

annotations.label: LCM as ideal intersection generator in a PID

anchor.snippet: the least common multiple of a and b can be characterised as a generator of the intersection of the ideals generated by a and b

annotations.note: span_singleton_inf_span_singleton proves span{n} ⊓ span{m} = span{lcm n m} for a EuclideanDomain GCDMonoid, a special case of the PID characterization.
